content:
Load Balancing

4 min read
Just now

--

If you’ve read the earlier parts of this series, welcome back. If this is your first stop, no worries you just need a rough idea of what distributed systems are, and we’ll build from there.
Let’s start with an analogy
Picture four siblings, all equally great at baking cookies, all working the same shop. Here’s the catch: only one person can serve a customer at a time. And for some reason, every customer who walks in ends up going to the same sibling, every time. That one sibling is running themselves ragged while the other three are just… standing there.
What do you do? You hire a middleman. Someone who stands between the siblings and the customers, checks who’s free or who’s least occupied, and sends the customer their way. Problem solved the work actually gets spread around now.
That middleman? That’s basically what a load balancer is, except instead of siblings and cookies, we’re talking about servers and requests.
Okay, so what is a load balancer, really
In any real system, a single server is a liability it’s a single point of failure. So the usual fix is to have more than one (replication, more resources, whatever you want to call it). But the moment you have multiple servers, you need someone deciding which server handles which request. That’s the load balancer’s job: spread the load across servers so none of them gets crushed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How does it actually decide where a request goes?
Every request that comes in has some detail attached to it maybe a username or an IP or something identifying. Let’s just call that the key.
The load balancer takes that key and runs it through a hashing algorithm. Hashing basically just means: give me any input, and I’ll give you back a unique value for it. So now we’ve got a hash value in hand. And since we know how many servers we’ve got, we can figure out which one this request should go to by doing:
server_index = hash(key) % number_of_serversThe modulo operator just squeezes that hash value down into a number that fits within our server count. And boom that number points to a server.
Simple enough. But like most simple things, it’s got a catch.
Here’s where it falls apart
Servers fail. That’s just a fact of life in distributed systems. And the moment a server drops out, your total server count changes which means that modulo calculation is now producing different results for almost every key. A request that used to land on Server 5 might now land somewhere totally different, even though it had nothing to do with why Server 5 went down.
So basically, one server failing causes a ripple effect where nearly everything gets reshuffled. That’s a lot of unnecessary churn expensive in time, expensive in resources, and honestly kind of a design failure once you notice it.
Now, you might be thinking the modulo calculation itself is cheap, so why does this actually matter? It’s not the math that’s the problem, it’s what remapping causes issues. Say this load balancer is routing to caches, Server 5 built up a nice warm cache for a bunch of users over time. The moment those keys remap to Server 2, that server has never seen them before, so its cache is cold and it has to go fetch everything from the database all over again for basically every key, all at once. One small failure shouldn’t cause that much disruption.
This is the part where I went “okay, there’s got to be a better way” and there is.
Enter consistent hashing
Instead of doing a modulo over your server count, imagine your servers sitting on a circle a ring. That’s the whole idea behind consistent hashing.
And here’s a neat detail: each server doesn’t just get one spot on that ring. It gets scattered across multiple spots as “virtual nodes.” So the ring might look something like: Server 1, Server 2, Server 1 again, Server 3, Server 4, Server 1 again… and so on around the circle. Why bother with multiple spots per server? Because if each server only had one point on the ring, you could easily end up with a lopsided ring most of the traffic clustering around one or two servers just by bad luck. Spreading each server across several points evens that out.
So how does routing actually work here?

• Hash the key, same as before deterministic, always gives you the same output for the same input.

• See where that hash lands on the ring.

• Walk clockwise from that point until you hit a node. Whichever server that node belongs to that’s who handles the request.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why this actually solves the failure problem
Here’s the payoff: if a server goes down, only the keys that were pointing to that servers nodes need to move. They just shift over to the next node clockwise on the ring. Everything else on the ring? Untouched. Doesn’t even know anything happened.
Compare that to the modulo approach, where one failure could scramble your entire mapping. Consistent hashing turns a “reshuffle everything” problem into a “move a small slice of things” problem which is exactly why you’ll find it used everywhere in real world distributed systems, from caching layers to databases.
That’s it for this part. Honestly, writing this out helped me understand it better than just reading about it did if I’ve gotten something wrong or oversimplified somewhere, I’d genuinely love to hear about it in the comments. This is a learning in public thing for me, not a “here’s the definitive answer” thing.
